category_title: Students with persistent challenging behavior referred for individualized behavior support
minimal_context: The student is participating in a large group language arts lesson where the class is instructed to read from their workbooks.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Class, please open your reading workbooks to page 20 so we can start our language arts lesson.

STUDENT: (Grabs the workbook from the student sitting next to them) I want to use yours.

TUTOR: Please return your classmate's workbook and open your own.

STUDENT: (Pushes the workbook off the desk onto the floor) No, I don't want to.

TUTOR: Pick up the workbook and turn to page 20.

STUDENT: (Stands up and shouts loudly across the room) Make me! I'm not doing this reading!

options.A: Mention reading or enjoying specific books to engage the student in the language arts lesson.
options.B: Grant full agency by telling the student they can just pick out any workbook page they like.
options.C: Joke with the student and laugh at oneself to defuse the situation during the reading lesson.
options.D: Use frequent opportunities to respond to maintain the student's orientation to the reading instruction.
